item.title: US Regulator Makes ‘Interventionist’ Pivot to Speed Data Centers
item.description: The chairman of the US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission said moves to help AI data centers accelerate hook ups to the country’s electric grids reflect a new and more interventionist approach by the regulator.

item.title: Trump Administration Backtracks on Removing Ocean Sensors
item.description: The U-turn comes after a plan to dismantle the monitors sparked opposition from scientists and members of Congress.

item.title: Over 100 Countries Back UN Framework for Climate Migration
item.description: More than 100 countries endorsed a new United Nations framework for communities so threatened by climate change that they may need to resettle elsewhere.

item.title: Climate Negotiators Reach Finance Sticking Point in Bonn Talks
item.description: Representatives at the United Nations talks couldn’t agree on how to pay to combat global warming.

item.title: Finance Fight Clouds UN Climate Talks Even as War Adds Momentum
item.description: Negotiators in Bonn got stuck on a familiar problem: how to find money to help developing nations tackle global warming.

item.title: US Eases Environmental Reviews for Natural Gas Projects
item.description: A top US energy regulator voted to ease environmental requirements for pipelines and other natural gas projects, saying they’re not needed after a recent Supreme Court ruling.

item.title: UK Government Eases Pressure on Tougher Green Building Upgrades
item.description: The UK has delayed rules that will require commercial landlords to meet stricter energy efficiency regulations for some of their buildings, giving building owners five years to meet the new standards while exempting smaller properties.

item.title: Pimco Targets Out-of-Date Assets in New Real Estate Strategy
item.description: Pimco Prime Real Estate is rolling out a new strategy that will target out-of-date buildings in major cities, with a view to making them more suited to a world increasingly shaped by extreme weather.

item.title: US Northeast Threatened by Severe Thunderstorms, Damaging Winds
item.description: More than 63 million Americans in a Northeast Corridor that spans Washington, through New York, to Boston are threatened by wild weather on Thursday, including severe thunderstorms and damaging winds.

item.title: US West Set to Sizzle This Summer While Northeast Stays Cooler
item.description: The US West and Gulf Coast are set to broil this summer while the rest of the nation may face dreary and cool days — thanks in part to El Niño.

item.title: The Energy Transition Debate We Need to Have
item.description: Jean-Baptiste Fressoz argues that current plans for a global switch to renewables are unrealistic — because they’re not ambitious enough.

item.title: Nvidia-Backed Startup Aims to Speed AI Data Center Grid Connections
item.description: Verse Enterprises Inc. is betting batteries and solar can help data centers skip the line.

item.title: European Heat Wave Threatens to Spur Record Cooling Demand
item.description: Western Europe is baking under a heat wave that threatens to drive record cooling demand, just as warming rivers force French nuclear reactors to curb output.

item.title: Top EU Party Wants Carbon Market Changes to Protect Industries
item.description: The biggest political group in the European Union’s parliament is considering pushing for carbon market changes to safeguard the region’s industrial competitiveness and curb excessive price swings.

item.title: Australia Sees Biggest Jump in Planned Large Renewable Projects
item.description: The amount of large-scale renewables projects classified as “probable” in Australia rose the most in records going back a decade, as the country pushes forward with ambitious plans to reduce emissions and hit green targets.

item.title: Data Centers Are Being Built in Areas Exposed to Extreme Weather
item.description: A significant number of data centers is being built in areas that are exposed to extreme weather risks brought on by climate change, a report by XDI has found.

item.title: India Monsoon Starts Almost 40% Short as El Niño Upends Weather
item.description: India’s monsoon has had an unusually weak start and the lackluster rainfall is likely to persist, threatening crops and industrial activity as the El Niño weather phenomenon takes hold.

item.title: Jera Books Mitsui OSK Ships to Bring Low-Carbon Ammonia to Japan
item.description: Jera Co. has agreed to book two gas carriers from Mitsui OSK Lines Ltd. that will enable Japan’s largest power producer to transport low-carbon ammonia from the US and build up a supply chain for the alternative fuel.

item.title: Three Rugby Deaths a Reminder of Extreme Heat Risks in Malaysia
item.description: The deaths in quick succession of three rugby players in Malaysia have put renewed focus on the risks of competing in extreme heat and humidity, as climate change pushes temperatures higher across Southeast Asia.

item.title: AI’s Growing Power Needs Are Driving Up Carbon Prices
item.description: As AI’s power demand surges, carbon prices rise and a startup launches a new geothermal plant design.

item.title: Invenergy Seeks Geothermal Leases as Trump Cancels Offshore Wind
item.description: Invenergy LLC is accelerating its acquisitions of US geothermal leases just as the Trump administration canceled four of the company’s offshore wind leases worth $765 million.

item.title: Data Center Alley Is Reshaping the Northeast Carbon Market
item.description: Virginia’s democratic governor is driving the state’s return to the market in a partisan flip on climate policy.

item.title: Trump to Pay Invenergy to Cancel Offshore Wind Power Leases
item.description: The Trump administration is canceling four offshore wind leases held by Invenergy LLC and refunding the privately held company $765 million, according a person familiar with the matter, the latest move by the White House to block new offshore wind development.

item.title: Tropical Storm Arthur Forms Off of Texas, Bringing Flooding Rain
item.description: Tropical Storm Arthur developed out of a swirl of thunderstorms and showers in the Gulf Coast, bringing flooding rain to Texas.

item.title: Hyperscalers to Get Clarity on How to Speed Access to US Grids
item.description: After months of uncertainty over how the US will accelerate the connection of data centers to electric grids, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is poised to provide long-awaited guidance on how to do so.

item.title: Geothermal Startup Bets Modular Plants Can Power AI Boom
item.description: Critical Energy plans to manufacture geothermal plants on an assembly line, cutting deployment timelines to weeks.

item.title: Botswana Eyes Power Exports With $100-Million Solar Plant
item.description: Botswana is set to begin construction of its first large-scale solar plant after developers secured $100 million in financing, as investors bet that rising electricity demand across southern Africa will create a lucrative market for surplus power.

item.title: Illinois, Indiana Brace for Thunderstorms While Floods Hit Texas
item.description: As the US Gulf coast braces for flooding, there’s a moderate level 4 risk of severe thunderstorms across central Illinois and Indiana.

item.title: Stripe-Led Group Deepens Carbon Removal Bet With $915 Million Fund
item.description: The collective, known as Frontier, pools money to buy carbon removal from startups developing durable climate solutions.

item.title: Dry June Forces Mumbai to Shut Taps for Construction Sites
item.description: Mumbai has suspended water supply to construction sites as a stalled monsoon and the worst June rain deficit in two decades risk slowing business activity in India’s financial hub.
